title: The Mortgage Rate Conundrum

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: Staff Report No. 829 from the Federal Reserve Bank of New York documents the emergence of a disconnect between mortgage and Treasury interest rates in summer 2003, following the end of the Federal Reserve's expansionary monetary cycle in June 2003. The research finds that mortgage rates failed to rise according to their historical relationship with Treasury yields, leading to significantly and persistently easier mortgage credit conditions. Using millions of loan-level observations to control for loan, borrower, and geographic characteristics, the authors reveal that delinquency rates began rising for loans originated after mid-2003, coinciding exactly with the period when mortgage rates disconnected from Treasury yields.
key_points: Mortgage rates failed to rise in line with Treasury yields following the Fed's rate-hike cycle ending June 2003
The disconnect led to significantly and persistently easier mortgage credit conditions
Research used a large dataset with millions of loan-level observations to control for loan, borrower, and geographic characteristics
Delinquency rates started rising for loans originated after mid-2003, exactly when the mortgage-Treasury relationship broke down
Authors are Alejandro Justiniano, Giorgio E. Primiceri, and Andrea Tambalotti
why_it_matters: This research documents how the historical relationship between mortgage rates and Treasury yields can break down, with significant implications for credit availability and credit risk. The disconnect between policy rates and mortgage market conditions may create unintended easing of credit that can contribute to building vulnerabilities in the financial system, as evidenced by the rising delinquency rates observed post-mid-2003.
market_structure_context: The paper examines the mortgage rate-Treasury yield relationship, a key component of the mortgage-backed securities market structure and pricing mechanism. The disconnect documented represents a structural shift in how monetary policy transmits to mortgage markets.
